turns.content: listen I need a recommendation on something. Marie wants new kitchen cabinets. we've had the same ones since we moved in and she's right they look like hell. what should I be looking at.

Kitchen cabinets are one of those things where the range is huge — you can spend $5K or $50K depending on what you go with. A few questions to narrow it down:

1. Are you looking at replacing all the cabinets, or just refacing the existing ones (new doors/hardware on the old boxes)?
2. What's the kitchen size — roughly how many cabinets are we talking?
3. Budget range — any number Marie has in mind or that you're working with?
4. Are you doing the install yourself or hiring it out?

full replacement. the boxes are 25 years old and they're falling apart. kitchen's not huge, it's a rowhouse. maybe 15 cabinets total upper and lower. I can do demo but I'm not hanging cabinets. I'll hire that out.

Smart — demo is easy money saved but cabinet install is worth paying for. Crooked cabinets will drive you crazy for the next 25 years.

For 15 cabinets in a rowhouse kitchen, here's the landscape:

**Budget-friendly ($3K–$6K for cabinets only):**
- RTA (Ready to Assemble) — brands like IKEA, Lily Ann, or Cabinets To Go. You assemble them, installer hangs them. IKEA's SEKTION line is actually solid quality for the price.

**Mid-range ($6K–$12K):**
- Semi-custom from places like KraftMaid, Diamond, or CliqStudios. More style options, better construction. These are plywood boxes with dovetail drawers — they'll last.

**High-end ($12K+):**
- Full custom. Probably overkill for a rowhouse kitchen unless Marie has very specific taste.

For a 25-year-old rowhouse kitchen, I'd lean mid-range. You'll get quality that lasts without going overboard. Does Marie have a style preference — shaker, flat panel, traditional raised panel?
she wants white shaker. she's been looking at pictures on her phone for about 6 months now so I know the decision is already made. I just gotta make it happen.

Ha — sounds like you've got your marching orders. White shaker is the most popular cabinet style going, so you'll have tons of options at every price point.

Here's my suggestion: go look at IKEA's SEKTION with the AXSTAD white doors. It's a clean white shaker look, quality is genuinely good (plywood boxes, not particle board), and for 15 cabinets you're probably looking at $3K–$4K for the cabinets themselves. Then budget $2K–$3K for a good installer. Total around $5K–$7K.

If Marie wants to step it up, KraftMaid does a white shaker that's a notch nicer — better soft-close hardware, more configuration options. You'd be closer to $8K–$10K all in with install.

Either way, white shaker in a rowhouse kitchen is going to look great. Want me to break down what to look for when comparing quotes from installers?

yeah that'd be helpful. Marie and I have been in this house 25 years. Fishtown. bought it when Fishtown was still cheap if you can believe that. place needs work but we're not going anywhere so might as well do it right.

Fishtown 25 years ago — you bought at the right time. That neighborhood has changed a lot. And yeah, if you're staying put, it's worth investing in the house.

For comparing installer quotes, here's what to look at:

**Get at least 3 quotes.** For a Philly rowhouse kitchen, installation should run $1,500–$3,000 depending on complexity.

**What the quote should include:**
- Demo and haul-away of old cabinets
- Installation of all uppers and lowers, including shimming and leveling (rowhouses are never level)
- Crown molding or trim if you want it
- Hardware installation
- Any filler pieces or scribe molding for walls that aren't plumb

**What to ask:**
- Are they insured? (Don't skip this)
- Do they handle the plumbing disconnect/reconnect for the sink, or do you need a separate plumber?
- Timeline — a 15-cabinet kitchen should be 2–3 days for install
- Do they warranty their work?

**Red flags:**
- Cash only, no contract
- Way cheaper than everyone else
- Can start "tomorrow" (good installers are booked out)

Want me to help you figure out timing? If Marie wants this done before the holidays you're already tight.
no holidays are off the table. she knows that. probably looking at January or February. Marie's realistic about this stuff. 32 years married, she knows how long things take in this house.

32 years — that's a solid run. And January or February is actually good timing. Contractors are slower in the winter so you'll have better luck getting someone in quickly and maybe negotiating a bit on price.

Here's a rough timeline to work backward from:

- **Now – December:** Pick cabinets and order them. If you go IKEA, they're usually in stock. Semi-custom like KraftMaid can take 4–6 weeks to arrive.
- **December:** Get your 3 installer quotes and book someone for January.
- **January/February:** Demo (you handle it), install (they handle it), done in a week total.

One tip — order 5–10% extra cabinet pieces if you can. Rowhouse walls are unforgiving and sometimes things need to be adjusted on site. Cheaper to have a spare filler or panel than to pause the job waiting for one to ship.

Are you planning to do anything with the countertops while you're at it, or just cabinets for now?
cabinets only. if I open the countertop can of worms she's going to want new countertops too and then it's a backsplash and then it never ends. one thing at a time.

Smart call — scope creep on a kitchen reno is real. Stick to the cabinets, get them done right, and revisit the rest later if you want. Once the new cabinets are in, the old countertops might not bother her as much as she thinks.
exactly. keep it contained. I run fire scenes the same way. don't open doors you don't have to. alright. I'll start looking this weekend. appreciate the breakdown.
Good plan. Take Marie with you to look at them in person — pictures on the phone never quite match the real thing. Good luck with the project.
Anytime. Let me know if you need help with anything else down the road.
